Name: dispersion_conditioned_metric
Title: Dispersion-Conditioned Metric Generation: Leveraging Support Set Statistics for Task-Adaptive Few-Shot Classification
Short Hypothesis: A lightweight hypernetwork conditioned on per-class prototype dispersion statistics (mean, variance, covariance trace) can generate task-specific diagonal Mahalanobis metrics that outperform fixed Euclidean and cosine distances in few-shot classification, especially on tasks with heterogeneous intra-class variance.
Related Work: Prototypical Networks (Snell et al., 2017, arXiv:1703.05175) use fixed Euclidean distance and noted that learning per-class variance did not help empirically. TADAM (Oreshkin et al., 2018, arXiv:1805.10123) conditions feature scaling on the task but does not generate the metric function itself. AMM (arXiv:2211.00890) fuses pre-defined distance metrics adaptively but does not generate novel metrics. GPAM (arXiv:2410.20320) models Gaussian prototypes for relation extraction but targets open relation extraction, not general few-shot classification with generated metrics. Free Lunch (arXiv:2101.06395) uses covariance for distribution calibration, not metric generation. Our approach differs by explicitly conditioning a metric-generating hypernetwork on dispersion statistics — the spread and shape of each class's support features — to produce a per-task diagonal scaling that adapts the distance function to the geometric structure of each episode.
Abstract: Metric-based few-shot learning methods classify query examples by their distance to class prototypes in a learned embedding space. Despite their simplicity and effectiveness, these methods universally apply a fixed distance function — typically Euclidean or cosine — across all tasks, ignoring the fact that different few-shot episodes exhibit vastly different intra-class variance patterns. We propose Dispersion-Conditioned Metric Networks (DCMNet), where a lightweight hypernetwork (fewer than 5,000 parameters) takes as input the dispersion statistics of each class's support embeddings — specifically the per-dimension variance and cross-dimension covariance trace — and outputs a task-specific diagonal scaling vector that modulates the distance computation. This transforms the standard Euclidean distance into a task-adaptive Mahalanobis-like metric without the computational cost of full matrix estimation. We train DCMNet end-to-end with episodic training on top of a frozen or jointly-trained ResNet-12 backbone. We evaluate on mini-ImageNet (5-way 1-shot and 5-shot), CIFAR-FS, and Omniglot, comparing against ProtoNet, TADAM, and the adaptive multi-distance method AMM. We further conduct ablations on conditioning inputs (variance only, covariance trace, prototype norms) and metric parameterizations (diagonal, low-rank, full). We hypothesize that dispersion conditioning yields the largest gains on tasks with high variance heterogeneity across classes, and provide analysis stratifying results by intra-class variance ratios.
Experiments: Experiment 1 (Main comparison): Train DCMNet with ResNet-12 backbone on mini-ImageNet 5-way 1-shot and 5-shot. Compare accuracy against ProtoNet (Euclidean), ProtoNet (cosine), TADAM, and a learned-fixed-diagonal baseline. Report mean accuracy and 95% confidence intervals over 600 episodes. Expected: 1-3% improvement over ProtoNet, competitive with TADAM but with far fewer parameters.

Experiment 2 (Cross-dataset generalization): Train on mini-ImageNet, evaluate zero-shot transfer on CIFAR-FS and Omniglot without fine-tuning the hypernetwork. Expected: dispersion-conditioned metrics generalize better than fixed metrics because they adapt to novel data statistics.

Experiment 3 (Ablation on conditioning inputs): Compare four conditioning variants — (a) per-class variance vector, (b) variance + covariance trace, (c) variance + prototype norm, (d) all statistics. Measure accuracy and hypernetwork parameter count. Expected: variance alone captures most of the benefit; covariance trace adds marginal gains.

Experiment 4 (Metric expressiveness ablation): Compare diagonal scaling, rank-4 low-rank matrix, and full matrix metric generation. Measure accuracy vs. parameter count trade-off. Expected: diagonal achieves best accuracy-efficiency trade-off; full matrix overfits in 1-shot.

Experiment 5 (Variance heterogeneity analysis): Stratify test episodes by the ratio of max-to-min class variance. Plot accuracy gain of DCMNet over ProtoNet as a function of this ratio. Expected: gains concentrate on high-heterogeneity episodes, validating the motivation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: The original ProtoNet paper noted that learning per-class variance did not help. Our approach differs (generating a shared task metric from dispersion stats, not per-class variance weighting), but the negative result suggests the embedding may already absorb variance information. Mitigation: test with both frozen and jointly-trained backbones.

Risk 2: In 1-shot settings, per-class variance estimates from a single example are undefined. Mitigation: use the task-level (all support examples pooled) variance instead of per-class variance in 1-shot, and evaluate whether this still provides useful signal.
Risk 3: The hypernetwork adds overhead to each episode. Mitigation: keep it under 5K parameters and benchmark inference time to confirm negligible overhead (<5% wall-clock increase).

Name: contrastive_metric_calibration
Title: Contrastive Metric Calibration: Learning to Separate Task-Specific Distance Functions for Few-Shot Classification
Short Hypothesis: Applying a contrastive objective at the metric-parameter level — pushing apart metrics that confuse similar classes and pulling together metrics that correctly discriminate — yields better-calibrated task-adaptive metrics than standard cross-entropy training alone.
Related Work: Meta Metric Learners (Yu et al., 2019, arXiv:1901.09890) learn task-specific metrics via a meta-learner but train with standard classification loss only. HyperShot (Sendera et al., 2022, arXiv:2203.11378) generates classifier weights via hypernetworks but does not apply contrastive learning on the generated parameters. AMM (arXiv:2211.00890) fuses multiple fixed metrics but does not learn to contrast metric configurations. Supervised contrastive learning (Khosla et al., 2020) operates on feature embeddings, not on metric parameters. Our key novelty is applying contrastive learning in metric-parameter space: we define a similarity between metric configurations and train a hypernetwork such that metrics generated for structurally similar tasks cluster together, while metrics for dissimilar tasks are pushed apart, improving metric generalization to novel tasks.
Abstract: Task-adaptive metric learning for few-shot classification typically trains a metric generator using only the classification loss on query examples. However, this provides no direct learning signal about how metric configurations should relate across different tasks. We propose Contrastive Metric Calibration (CMC), a training framework where a lightweight hypernetwork generates per-task diagonal metric vectors, and an auxiliary contrastive loss operates in metric-parameter space. Specifically, we sample pairs of episodes during training, compute a task similarity score based on their support set statistics, and apply an InfoNCE-style loss that pulls together the generated metric vectors for similar tasks while pushing apart those for dissimilar tasks. This regularizes the hypernetwork to produce a smooth, structured mapping from task characteristics to metric configurations, improving generalization to novel tasks at test time. We implement CMC on top of ProtoNet with a 4-layer ConvNet backbone for efficiency. Experiments on CIFAR-FS and mini-ImageNet demonstrate that the contrastive metric loss provides consistent improvements over both fixed metrics and metric generators trained with classification loss alone. We analyze the learned metric space via t-SNE visualization of generated metric vectors across thousands of episodes, revealing interpretable clustering structure aligned with task difficulty and class similarity.
Experiments: Experiment 1 (Main comparison on CIFAR-FS): Train CMC with 4-layer ConvNet on CIFAR-FS 5-way 1-shot and 5-shot. Compare against ProtoNet, ProtoNet + hypernetwork (classification loss only), and TADAM. Report accuracy over 600 episodes. Expected: CMC improves over classification-only hypernetwork by 1-2%, showing the value of the contrastive metric loss.
Experiment 2 (mini-ImageNet evaluation): Same setup on mini-ImageNet with ResNet-12 backbone. Expected: consistent gains, potentially larger in 1-shot where metric quality matters more.

Experiment 3 (Ablation on contrastive loss weight): Vary the weight λ of the contrastive metric loss from 0.0 to 2.0. Plot accuracy vs. λ. Expected: optimal λ around 0.3-0.5; too high collapses metric diversity.

Experiment 4 (Task similarity function ablation): Compare three task similarity definitions — (a) support set feature cosine similarity, (b) prototype distance, (c) class label overlap (for tasks from the same meta-training classes). Expected: feature-based similarity works best as it captures structural similarity.

Experiment 5 (Metric space visualization): Generate metric vectors for 2000 random episodes, apply t-SNE, and color by task properties (average intra-class variance, number of fine-grained classes). Expected: interpretable clusters emerge, validating that CMC learns structured metric spaces.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Defining task similarity is non-trivial — poor similarity functions could provide noisy contrastive signal. Mitigation: test multiple similarity definitions and fall back to classification-only training if contrastive loss hurts.

Risk 2: Sampling episode pairs doubles the per-step memory cost. Mitigation: use a metric-vector memory bank (store recent metric vectors) instead of computing pairs on-the-fly, reducing memory to near-baseline levels.

Risk 3: The contrastive loss may over-regularize small hypernetworks, reducing their capacity to differentiate tasks. Mitigation: ablate hypernetwork size (1K to 20K params) and contrastive loss weight jointly.

Name: low_rank_metric_ensemble
Title: Attention-Weighted Low-Rank Metric Ensembles for Few-Shot Classification
Short Hypothesis: Generating an ensemble of K low-rank metric components via a hypernetwork — combined through cross-attention weights derived from query-support interactions — captures richer task structure than any single generated metric, improving few-shot accuracy with minimal parameter overhead.
Related Work: AMM (arXiv:2211.00890) proposes adaptive fusion of multiple pre-defined distance metrics (Euclidean, cosine, Manhattan) but does not generate the component metrics. TADAM (arXiv:1805.10123) generates a single task-conditioned feature transformation. HyperShot (arXiv:2203.11378) generates classifier weights but uses a single kernel function. Meta Metric Learners (arXiv:1901.09890) learn a single task-specific metric via meta-learning. Our approach uniquely combines metric generation with ensembling: a hypernetwork produces K diverse low-rank metric components (each rank-r, where r << embedding dimension), and cross-attention between query and support embeddings determines the mixing weights. This allows different query examples within the same task to use different metric weightings, enabling fine-grained per-query adaptation.
Abstract: Metric-based few-shot learning relies on a single distance function to compare query examples against class prototypes. While recent work has explored task-adaptive metrics, all existing approaches generate or select a single metric per task. We argue that within a single few-shot episode, different query examples may benefit from different distance computations — for instance, a query near the decision boundary may need a metric emphasizing discriminative dimensions, while a confident query benefits from a general-purpose metric. We propose Low-Rank Metric Ensembles (LoRME), where a compact hypernetwork generates K low-rank metric components (each parameterized as a rank-r outer product, totaling K×r×d parameters per task where d is the embedding dimension). For each query, cross-attention between the query embedding and the support set embeddings produces K attention-derived weights that combine the metric components into a query-specific distance function. This per-query metric adaptation goes beyond per-task adaptation while maintaining efficiency: with K=4 and r=2 on a 512-d embedding, the hypernetwork adds only ~4K parameters. We evaluate LoRME on mini-ImageNet and CIFAR-FS with both ConvNet-4 and ResNet-12 backbones. We conduct ablations on the number of components K, the rank r, and the attention mechanism (cross-attention vs. self-attention vs. uniform weighting). We expect LoRME to particularly benefit hard episodes with ambiguous queries near class boundaries.
Experiments: Experiment 1 (Main comparison): Train LoRME with ResNet-12 on mini-ImageNet 5-way 1-shot and 5-shot. Compare against ProtoNet (Euclidean), ProtoNet (cosine), AMM, TADAM, and a single-metric hypernetwork baseline. Report accuracy and 95% CI over 600 episodes. Expected: 1-2% gain over single-metric hypernetwork, 2-4% over fixed ProtoNet.

Experiment 2 (CIFAR-FS with ConvNet-4): Repeat with lightweight 4-layer ConvNet backbone on CIFAR-FS to test feasibility on minimal compute. Expected: gains persist but are smaller due to lower-dimensional embeddings (64-d).

Experiment 3 (Ablation on K and r): Sweep K ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8} and r ∈ {1, 2, 4, 8}. Plot accuracy vs. total metric parameters (K×r×d). Expected: K=4, r=2 is the sweet spot; K=1 degrades to single metric; K=8 overfits in 1-shot.

Experiment 4 (Attention mechanism ablation): Compare (a) cross-attention (query attends to support), (b) self-attention on query only, (c) uniform weights (K components averaged equally). Expected: cross-attention > self-attention > uniform, validating that query-support interaction drives metric selection.

Experiment 5 (Per-query analysis): For 200 episodes, record per-query metric weights and query difficulty (distance to nearest wrong-class prototype). Plot metric weight distribution as a function of query difficulty. Expected: hard queries use more diverse metric weightings; easy queries converge to near-uniform weights.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Risk 1: Low-rank components with small r may not span a sufficiently expressive metric space. Mitigation: include a residual connection to the base Euclidean distance so the ensemble only needs to learn corrections.

Risk 2: Cross-attention at query time adds inference overhead proportional to K × |support set|. Mitigation: benchmark wall-clock time; with K=4 and 5-way 5-shot, this is only 100 attention computations per query, negligible compared to backbone forward pass.
Risk 3: Per-query metric adaptation may introduce instability during meta-training. Mitigation: warm-start with uniform weights for the first 10 epochs, then gradually enable the attention mechanism.
